danger_signals.visual_form: Proximity-based visual spacing of opponent players on court
danger_signals.meaning: Indicates risk of turnover or blocked shot; player is in danger of losing possession

danger_signals.visual_form: Player animation speed reduction and visual pacing changes
danger_signals.meaning: Character is in danger of reduced performance; stamina depleted

safety_signals.visual_form: Isometric overhead view shows space between player and nearest defender
safety_signals.meaning: Safe passing lane or clear offensive opportunity available

safety_signals.visual_form: Button state indicator for temporary speed burst mechanic
safety_signals.meaning: Player can safely execute evasive movement to escape defensive pressure

faction_indicators.color_identity: Red and yellow team coloring (historical NBA uniform)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opposing team rendered in alternate uniform colors on same court
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Court-side benches, coaching areas, and scoreboard displays team affiliation

faction_indicators.color_identity: Blue and orange team coloring (historical NBA uniform)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Opposing team rendered in alternate uniform colors on same court
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Court-side benches, coaching areas, and scoreboard displays team affiliation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 30-degree-overhead isometric court view showing all players in real-time positioning; continuous player animation and ball movement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Players execute offensive and defensive formations; ball carrier can use turbo button for speed burst; defenders close gaps

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ball control indicator shifts between teams; court positioning resets to reflect new team's offensive setup
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Former defensive team transitions to offensive role; formations swap based on roster management

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lineup interface shows starting player roster; limited trading mechanics display available player swaps
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can substitute starters; only starting lineup eligible for trades (Genesis-specific limitation)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Split or shared court view; dual player input indicators
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Multiple players control different team members simultaneously; coordinators noted as confusing in contemporary reviews

environmental_storytelling.example: Cover features action shot from 1994 NBA Finals between Houston Rockets and New York Knicks, establishing temporal and competitive context

Genesis version features 1994-95 rosters (up-to-date); SNES version features 1993-94 rosters; PC version includes rookie class and trade deadline updates. Roster differences communicate game's knowledge of real-world NBA timeline

30-degree-overhead isometric view provides strategic information about player positioning and movement options; described as allowing 'better view of the action while allowing the game to run more smoothly'

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Turbo button grants temporary speed burst to escape defenders; animation pacing increases
Proximity to ball carrier causes reduced passing accuracy; players attempt interceptions and blocks
Movement speed visibly reduces; performance efficiency decreases

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Player in role (coach or GM mode)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can command roster changes, lineups, and trades (starting players only in Genesis version)

notes: NBA Live 95 uses sports simulation paradigm where faction identity equals team allegiance. Danger/safety signals derive from gameplay mechanics rather than narrative peril. World-state communication relies on isometric perspective, real-time roster accuracy, and role-switching (player/coach/GM). The game establishes world-state through authentic NBA roster data matched to real 1994-95 season timeline. No supernatural or combat danger exists; danger is purely competitive (turnover risk, poor positioning, fatigue). Safety signals center on mechanical advantages (open court, available turbo). Environmental storytelling minimal; primarily achieved through cover art and roster authenticity.
